title: Chapter 4
text: Chapter One L ara grasped the edges of the large board and heaved. Rock, dirt, and debris tumbled from it as it rose. She shoved it backward. It kicked up a thick cloud of dust when it landed, which was, swiftly carried away by the wind. Crouching over the exposed rubble — bits of splintered wood, rusted metal, and broken ceramic — she sifted through it with her fingers . “Well, what did you think you’d find?” she asked herself, sitting back on her heels, and ran her gaze over the nearby crumbled buildings. The ruins of another world surrounded her, blanketed in a thick layer of dirt . A drop of sweat trickled between her breasts. Though the cloth wrapped around her head and body protected her skin from the sun, it couldn’t keep out the heat. If it weren’t for the stinging bits of sand carried on the frequent guests of wind, she’d tear the sweat-dampened fabric off . “What the hell’s left to find in this trash heap ?” Her sister’s voice replied from the depths of her memory . There’s always something to find, so let’s make it a game. Whoever finds the best treasure gets a larger portion . Tabitha had always been good at distracting Lara in little ways, suggesting games and competitions to mask the truth of their situation. And, regardless of who found the best items, she always gave Lara the larger share of food. Whether Lara liked it or not, scavenging was the only way to find things the bots deemed valuable. Otherwise, she’d have nothing to eat at all . “Damn bots.” Lara swiped at the debris, knocking aside pebbles and dust. Thinking about Tabitha sent a worried pang through her chest; she hadn’t seen her sister in almost two months . She picked through the rubble, tossing every bit of metal she found into her bag. The pieces were small, and in poor shape, but they’d at least be worth something . Still, after hours in the relentless sun, she’d hoped for something more significant . The soft clink of a rock against a piece of ceramic gave her pause. She picked up one of the shards and turned it over. Though faded with time and wear, the blue pattern on it was still visible — it was part of a flower. Carefully, she brushed away the loose layer of dirt atop the ground, revealing more pieces with the same coloring . Once, each piece had been part of a larger whole. Bowls, plates, and cups, each beautiful in its own right. What good were they now? They weren’t worth the dust they lay in . Lara was about to toss the large shard back, but the pattern caught her eye again. She’d only ever seen flowers in pictures, and even those were difficult to find. She tucked the shard into a fold of cloth around her waist, away from anything that might damage it further . More digging yielded a few more items; two rusted forks and three spoons joined the scrap in her bag. Moving aside a smaller plank of rotted wood, she found a solid piece of glass. She tugged on it. The resistance told her it was much larger than she’d thought . Using a flat rock, she dug around the glass carefully, her excitement growing as more of it was revealed. Finally, she pulled it free. She held the heavy pitcher in her hands, amazed; the glass was foggy and scratched, intact save for a small chip near its base. It had a smooth, curved handle, and the bumps on the outside were raised depictions of leaves and flowers in flowing forms . It was beautiful . Wadding the end of her sleeve in her hand, she rubbed some of the dirt away so the glass caught the sun. It sparkled with an array of color, like a rainbow, the hues shifting as she turned it back and forth . Standing, she removed a length of cloth from her waist and wrapped it around the pitcher before placing the bundle in her bag. She picked it up and held it to her chest. One little slip, and the whole thing might shatter. Without a doubt, it was the best thing she’d ever found, and she was tempted to keep it for herself. But empty stomach wouldn’t allow that . She picked her way through the rubble, back to the wide stretch of dirt and scrub grass that served as a road. The pitcher would be worth something to Kate and Gary. Between them and their five-year-old daughter, Maggie, they always needed extra water on hand . Only their generosity that kept Lara from starvation. Because they were a family, their allotment of produce was higher, and they usually had a little to spare for Lara when she had items worth trading. She didn’t care for the pity in their eyes when they spoke with her, but hunger outweighed pride. Still, they had been kind since Tabitha’s disappearance, and she knew Gary dealt with her fairly. That he received more for her items at the Market than he gave her was understandable . The loss was acceptable, if it meant she didn’t have to deal with a bot ever again . Lara hurried down the road, glancing occasionally at the ruins to either side. A few of the walls were still upright, defying time and nature, sometimes teetering in the wind. The buildings here hadn’t been built to last; wood and crumbly plaster couldn’t compare to the brick and concrete structures in the Market . At the three-way intersection, she glanced up at the signpost. Tabitha had shown it to her when they were children. Lara couldn’t read the beat-up sign hanging from it, but she recognized the letters. She’d asked her sister what the letters meant , once . The way home . She followed the north road, back toward the collection of shacks that the humans of Cheyenne dwelled in . The return trip wasn’t long, but heat and hunger slowed Lara’s pace. The flattened ruins left her exposed to the wind and the biting dust it carried. She kept her head down and adjusted the cloth over her face. She’d be picking grit out of the most uncomfortable places for days . The landscape changed as she reached the outskirts of town; the dirt, scrub grass, and rubble slowly gave way to small, rickety buildings that creaked in the wind. Some of the structures had been standing for longer than anyone could remember, repaired with whatever scraps were available as the years passed. It wasn’t uncommon for a few of the shacks to fall apart whenever one of the frequent dust storms blew through, but the inhabitants just gathered the pieces and rebuilt . The shacks, spread out on the edges of the settlement, were packed more densely as she walked. With the increase in buildings came the stench — refuse and human waste, so potent she considered turning back and heading into the wastes every time she smelled it anew. She turned onto one of the northward roads — little more than a narrow patch of cracked, hard-packed dirt. It would be mud within minutes of the first rain . To the west, the sun approached the horizon. The people she passed were finishing up their daily business. Better to be indoors before nightfall. The cold was one thing, but no one wanted to be caught outside by a bored, wandering gearhead . She saw a few of the marks painted on the shacks she passed — a round gear fashioned to look like a skull. Warlord’s symbol. On bots, it meant they were in his service; on a human’s home, it meant they’d broken the rules. The first time didn’t usually result in death, but the shacks with his mark were often dark and quiet . Gary and Kate resided close to Lara’s home. She hurried to their shack, cradling her bag like it was the most precious thing in the world. Glancing up and down the street, she knocked on their door . “Who is it?” The muffled voice belonged to Gary . “It’s Lara,” she replied, pulling the cloth down from her face . The door pulled back slightly and slid to the side. Gary stood in the opening, a tall, thin man whose head nearly touched the top of the doorway. He wasn’t old — he was probably in his early thirties — but years of hardship and scavenging in Cheyenne’s unforgiving weather had aged him. Despite the harsh lines on his face and his thick beard, he had kind eyes . “Everything all right?” he asked . “You won’t believe what I found.” Lara opened her bag and pulled out the pitcher . “ Lara , wait — ” “It would be perfect for you and Kate, and little Maggie .” “Lara, we need to talk — ” “Isn’t it beautiful?” Lara removed the cloth and held the pitcher out to him. “Have you ever seen anything like it ?” She bit her lip as Gary stared at it, her arms aching as seconds passed. Surely it was worth more than a strip of dried meat or a single vegetable . Finally, Gary raised his gaze to meet hers, guilt and pity clear in his furrowed brow. Lara’s stomach sank . “You could hold a day’s worth of water in this,” she explained, turning the pitcher to display the inside. “Or…or food. You could keep — ” “Lara, we can’t trade anymore .” She lowered her arms, staring at him, silenced by her disbelief. Had he misspoken, or had she misheard him? They’d helped her without hesitation during Tabitha’s absence. It wasn’t much, but it kept her going. Kept her out of the Market . “What did you say?” she asked, clutching the pitcher to her chest . “I’m sorry, Lara. We’re sorry. But with Maggie getting bigger, it’s getting harder to spare the extra .” “Gary, please. Just…just think about it.” She tilted the heavy pitcher and leaned over to look past him. “Can I talk to Kate ?” “No, Lara. Maggie’s sleeping right now. Please, don’t make this any harder. We want to help you, we really do, but we have to consider our chil — ” Lara shoved past him. He stumbled aside, stammering as Lara entered the small living space . “Kate, please, don’t — ” Lara stilled. Kate, wide-eyed, held her arms over her rounded stomach, as though she could hide it . “Kate,” Lara breathed. They all knew the rule — one child per human couple . Gary swore, dragged the door back into place, and moved in front of Lara. He grasped her arms with strong, rough fingers, and bent down to eye level . “No one can know, Lara. No one !” he rasped . “I’d never tell anyone. But…how are you going to keep this secret ?” “We don’t know.” Kate stopped beside Gary, placing a hand on his shoulder . Gary swallowed, throat bobbing, and released Lara . “It’s getting harder to conceal now, and we aren’t earning as much with me having to stay hidden. Maggie doesn’t even know, but she’s been asking questions.” She grasped Gary’s hand. “We can’t let the gearheads find out, but we have nowhere else to go .” “We couldn’t tell anyone, not even you,” Gary said, his frown making the lines on his face harsher. “Kate and the baby need all they can get. We can’t spare anymore, not even in trade .” Lara studied Kate and knew he was right; she had dark circles under her eyes — in stark contrast to her pale skin — and her cheeks were hollow . “We’re sorry, Lara. We wish we could , but …” “I know,” Lara said, nodding. “I understand .” She was numb as Gary released Kate’s hand and opened the door. Two times in as many months, Lara’s world was falling apart. Passing through the doorway, she paused and turned to look at Gary and Kate. They’d helped Lara in her time of need, selflessly, and likely at great cost to their little family. It was more than anyone else would have done. More than they should have done . “Here.” Lara held the pitcher out to Gary . His eyebrows lifted. “No. We can’t accept that.” Behind him, Kate covered her mouth with her hand . “Please, just take it.” Her grip tightened on the handle. There were at least a dozen other people on the street who were likely to trade food for it, but who needed it more now than these two? “As a thank you. For all you’ve both done for me .” Kate’s eyes watered as Gary hesitantly accepted the offering. Lara ignored the pang of regret as she released it, busying her hands with replacing the cloth over her mouth and nose. “ Be safe .” “And you,” Kate said, voice thick . Lara walked away, feeling oddly heavy despite her empty hands . Her home stood on the northern edge of the human slums. Only the wide road — dirt and gravel, with long, rusted rails jutting from it at various points — and the wall separated her from the bot district, where the lights were already coming on. If they’d the means, she would’ve convinced Tabitha to move as far away from the wall as possible a long time ago . Lara’s shack was a mix of various materials, just like all the others. The base was brick, scavenged from a nearby building that collapsed when Lara was young. The wood boards that formed the walls had come from the same place. It was topped with a piece of sheet metal. She’d always enjoyed the pattering of rain and sand on the roof during storms . The chimes hanging near the entrance jingled in the wind. She’d used thin but durable lines — fishing line, Tabitha had said, though Lara didn’t know of any fish nearby — to hang a mismatched collection of spoons, forks, knives, and keys from a metal ring. The music they created was random, but there was nothing else like it in the settlement. She was surprised they hadn’t been stolen for scrap; it was likely because no one wanted to be in the shadow of the wall for long . She slid the door aside and entered, tossing her bag onto her pallet. She left a gap when she closed the door; a bit more airflow would help release some of the day’s heat . Removing her head wrap, she dropped it into a pile of spare cloth and moaned at the feel of cool air on her skin. She pulled the pin from her hair and let her braid fall free. Working her hair loose, she ran her fingers through it, closing her eyes as she massaged her scalp. Despite having been covered, dirt had worked its way in. If water weren’t so precious, she’d rinse her hair out . Inhaling deeply, she opened her eyes and lowered her hands . She walked across the small room, unfolded the sash at her waist, and removed the porcelain shard and her beat-up metal lighter. The latter was one of her most valuable items, one she refused to trade. Fuel wasn’t easy to come by, but having easy access to fire had saved Lara and Tabitha more than once during the frigid winters . Though the shadows inside were thickening, she found her lantern easily; it was always in the same place. After a few attempts, the lighter sparked to flame, and she lit the wick, filling the shack with a gentle, orange glow. The simple lantern — just a glass container with some oil and a wick through the lid — was one of her only luxuries. She probably used it too often, but it burned slow and ensured she didn’t have to be alone in the darkness . Returning the lighter to its place, she studied the porcelain again, turning it over in her hand. She traced the design with her fingertip, marveling at the way the looping lines came together to create something so beautiful. What purpose did it serve? None. It simply…was. Like so many of the remnants she’d found of the old world, the decoration had no practical use, couldn’t have aided in the item’s function . For some reason, such items spoke to her. She knew they were frivolous. A waste of her time and energy to collect. But Lara couldn’t resist . She placed the shard on the uppermost shelf of her little case, amidst the other pointless trinkets she’d gathered over the years — all items that weren’t worth anyone’s time to steal. They were pleasing to Lara’s eye, and that was enough . When she turned away from her treasures to look upon the rest of her home, she was reminded of how little she’d accomplished today. There was the pathetic pallet upon which she slept, and the wooden crate beside it with her few articles of clothing. Hunger reintroduced itself, gnawing at her gut. There was no food here . Even the hollowness of her stomach wasn’t enough to distract Lara from the true emptiness inside her. She was alone. Tabitha was her only friend, her only family. Her big sister, mother, best friend. Tabitha had guided Lara, taught her, comforted her. And now… Now, there was no one . She had nothing . With a cry of frustration, she sat down on the sturdy crate and yanked off her worn boots. Rising, she lifted the crate, stuffed her boots beneath it, and slammed it back down. For a long time, she sat hunched forward, breathing heavily as she picked at the wooden planks with her fingers. Her body shook with the effort of keeping her tears at bay. Crying couldn’t help anything. It certainly wouldn’t earn her any food . From outside, the soft sound of the chime rose above the constant drone of the wind. Lara’s muscles eased as the music chased away her sorrow and frustration. Anger wouldn’t help any more than crying . She closed her eyes and listened to the music, conjuring a melody in her mind. Her body swayed with the notes. She couldn’t ease her hunger, but she could free her mind for a little while . Lara hummed, the sound filling her chest and lifting her heart. Standing, she lifted her arms and slid her fingers into her hair. She rocked her hips in time with the beat in her head, letting the music take her away. Letting her forget . She stepped forward blindly, knowing in her heart exactly where everything lay inside the shack. The worn cloth of her skirt brushed her legs, twisting and flaring with her movement. Night air swept through the doorway, caressing her flushed cheeks with a brisk, fleeting kiss . Lara danced, not once opening her eyes in fear she would be torn from her dreams and dragged back into a world of hunger, depravity, and dust .
